Okay, onboarding. Let's talk about our four hours. Okay? Four hours are literally just a four part job description. Okay? So it's nothing special. It's the job description itself. It's just a format that works for us. Okay? Part one or the first R is the role. So this is an overview of the position or job description. It's going to summarize the tasks needed for this person to be successful in the position. I like to look at this as it's like my verbal sales pitch like this is what you'll be doing in a paragraph or less, right? Responsibilities. These are all the tasks involved in performing that role. So you basically list everything necessary to perform the job and you keep it as clear as possible. And so I make it very specific to our business. I'll include vendor names, departments, department position names, you know, whatever it is that they're touching, make it... Don't make it general. Make it very specific to your business. So I don't want you to think, well, if someone's looking from the outside and they don't know what I'm talking about, like they'll know enough to understand those are internal terms. You just need to make sure that when they come on and they're doing their job and they learn those things, they know exactly what's expected of them. Results. So what are the expected accomplishments of performing the job? Okay? So oftentimes these are KPIs that you're tracking. Maybe it's, you know, make... I don't know... Sell 50 challenges a month. Upsell 45 EFTs a month, you know, sell X amount of procedural lab supplements, whatever it may be. Those should be things under their results, okay? And sometimes it kind of helps to think if this job didn't exist, what would not be happening, right? Like say it's somebody that's over marketing or they're for you. If you weren't there, what would not be happening? Leads would not be coming in. Okay. What is the result needed from you? Leads should be coming in. Okay. So think about it like that too. And then lastly is requirements. And so requirements are the expected skills, requirements of the applicants they may consider for the job. So a lot of times these include degrees and years of experience, but I challenge you and I encourage you to go beyond that. I would say, you know, are they efficient with technology? D-suite using iPhone iOS. Are they quick to learn? Are they a problem solver? Are they able to confront issues, able to delegate, you know, whatever is needed for that position. Think of what that person, the ideal person for this role, what are they good at doing and that should be in the requirements.
